experiments.title: How gift array options in a high urgency sticky bar impact donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a gift array to the HUC sticky bar for IMB website visitors will increase engagement and donor conversion by minimizing the number of steps on the donation page, reducing friction and decision-making, resulting in more gifts.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/15/2026 - 01/19/2026
During IMB’s 2026 Winter Olympics campaign, we re-tested the sticky bar’s gift array buttons to understand their impact on donor behavior.
In reviewing the original execution, we identified opportunities to improve how the sticky bar guided visitors toward giving—particularly around campaign value proposition and giving array amounts. We re-tested this approach to determine whether modifying the sticky bar could more effectively encourage gifts and to gather data needed to inform future fundraising tactics.
For this test, 50% of site traffic was shown the standard sticky bar experience featuring a single call-to-action button. The remaining 50% received a treatment version with three buttons mirroring the suggested gift amounts on the donation page, allowing visitors to engage with a giving option earlier in their journey.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment shows that adding a gift array to the IMB website’s sticky bar significantly increased both visitor engagement and donor conversion. The treatment version—featuring three suggested gift amount buttons—outperformed the standard sticky bar by 448.4% in Clicks for All Traffic, with 99.9% statistical confidence.
These results suggest that introducing giving options earlier in the donor journey reduces friction and simplifies decision-making on the donation page—leading to more completed gifts.
Downstream Impact on Donations:
To understand how increased engagement translated into giving behavior, we analyzed downstream donation metrics. During the test period, the control version generated 1 gift, while the treatment generated 16 gifts, representing a 1,495% increase in transactions (100% LOC).
This confirms that the gift array not only drives more clicks but also meaningfully motivates visitors to complete a donation.
Gift Amount Performance:
We also evaluated which gift array buttons led to the most donations:
$25 Button: 6 gifts, all at $25
 	$50 Button: 3 gifts, all at $50
 	$100 Button: 3 gifts (2 at $100, 1 at $150)
 	More Options: 4 gifts, resulting in varied amounts ($20, $50, $50, $200)
Notably, most donors retained the amount they selected in the sticky bar through to the completion of the donation, reinforcing the value of early commitment.
Conclusion & Recommendation:
Overall, the gift array on the sticky bar effectively removes a key decision point from the donation page, leading to higher engagement and more completed gifts.
For future testing, IMB should continue exploring gift arrays and similar friction-reducing elements in high-visibility areas of the site. Ongoing iteration will help further optimize the giving experience and maximize donor conversion and revenue.

experiments.title: How removing a form disclaimer from a content offer landing page impacts sign up rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing the form disclaimer for 2026 Calendar landing page visitors will increase sign-up rates by eliminating unnecessary friction and reducing distractions, thereby preventing users from being drawn away from the form and allowing them to focus solely on completing their request.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/20/2026 - 02/03/2026
Each year, IMB runs a Calendar acquisition offer that consistently performs well. The campaign follows a standard funnel: users land on a page to request the calendar, then are redirected to a donation page to complete their gift.
This year, we noticed a new addition to the landing page—a small form disclaimer that was not present last year. Although subtle, the change raised concerns about whether it might be creating friction and negatively impacting conversion.
To evaluate its effect, we ran an A/B test over a two-week period, splitting traffic evenly between the version with the disclaimer and the version without it.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that removing the form disclaimer from the landing page for the 2026 Calendar acquisition offer led to a 10.1% increase in Email Acquisition for All Traffic. This change was statistically significant at a 100.0% confidence level, indicating a clear impact on user behavior. Moving forward, it's important to remember that reducing unnecessary friction and distractions can improve conversion rates. By simplifying the user experience and allowing visitors to focus solely on completing their request, we can potentially see higher engagement and sign-up rates. In future experimentation, it may be beneficial to continue testing different elements on the landing page to further optimize the conversion process. This could include testing different forms of messaging, images, layouts, or additional features to see what resonates best with users. By consistently running experiments and analyzing results, we can continue to improve the effectiveness of our campaigns and drive better outcomes for our nonprofit.

experiments.title: How quantifying the time investment in a high urgency email appeal impacts clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe quantifying the time investment for donors will increase the click-through rate because more people will see it as within reach.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/17/2025 - 12/18/2025
The IMB was running a donor survey as part of their year end campaign. They knew that people generally enjoyed giving their opinions, but wondered whether many thought it might take too long and therefore wouldn’t click.
The survey was short, but that couldn’t be seen in the email. They decided to run an experiment with a treatment that explicitly stated the survey would take “two minutes” to help donors answer the mental question: “Do I have time for this?” and see if it impacted click-through rate from the email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that quantifying the time investment for donors by stating that the survey would only take "two minutes" led to a 6.2% increase in clickthrough rate from the email. This indicates that explicitly mentioning the survey's short duration positively affected recipients' decision to engage with it.
In future experimentation, it would be valuable to continue testing the effectiveness of providing specific details, such as time investment, to reduce donors' perceived effort. This approach can increase engagement and ultimately improve the success of campaigns or initiatives designed to elicit responses from the target audience. Additionally, it is important to incorporate similar strategies across other communication channels to further enhance donor participation and interaction.

experiments.title: Will donors be more likely to give when their gift funds a specific, tangible outcome rather than a broader appeal?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the tangible ask for Deaflympics 2025 will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because it gave donors a clear, tangible way to fund the ministry side of the trip rather than the logistics of getting missionaries there.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/22/2025
IMB is raising funds for the 2025 Deaflympics in Tokyo to send sign-language-fluent missionaries who can share the Gospel with the Deaf community—one of the world’s most underserved groups.
We tested two approaches for our kickoff housefile email: a broad appeal highlighting the need to send missionaries versus a tactical ask where donors could directly fund Olympic pins to spark Gospel conversations. We anticipated the treatment would resonate more, since it gave donors a clear, tangible way to fund the ministry side of the trip rather than the logistics of getting missionaries there.
We wanted to know if donors are more likely to give when their gift supports a specific, tangible outcome (pins for Gospel conversations) rather than a broad appeal for trip costs.
Note: The control used more urgent language, which may have influenced results.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the broad appeal highlighting the overall need to send missionaries for the 2025 Deaflympics in Tokyo was more effective in generating donations compared to the tactical ask to fund Olympic pins. Despite the hypothesis that the tangible ask would increase donor conversion metrics by providing a clear and tangible way to fund the ministry side of the trip, the results showed a 58.5% decrease in donations for the treatment group.
Furthermore, the average gift size in the control was $112 while the treatment was $33, indicating that the control led to not only more gifts but also larger gifts.
It is important to note that this was not an entirely clean test. It could indicate that the pin-focused language was less compelling or too confusing for IMB's audience.
In future experimentation, it is essential to consider the effectiveness of different messaging strategies and appeals when soliciting donations. It may be beneficial to continue testing various approaches to understand donor preferences and behavior better. Additionally, conducting surveys or focus groups with donors could be valuable in gathering feedback on their motivations for giving and preferences for communication. This information can help tailor future campaigns to better resonate with donors and improve donation outcomes.

experiments.title: How the addition of matching language on a donation page impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the addition of the matching language for Non-Donors to the IMB will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because new donors will feel their impact is doubled, creating both urgency and greater perceived value for their donation.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/11/2025 - 09/16/2025
IMB has been raising support this fall for the Dealympics project. Recently, we learned about a special matching challenge available for new donors to IMB. To maximize its impact, we wanted to test using this match as an incentive—positioning it as the perfect opportunity for someone’s very first gift to IMB.
For this test, the control followed our standard copy approach, while the treatment added language about the new donor match.
*This test was only shown to Non-Donors
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding language about a matching challenge for new donors actually led to a decrease in transactions, including a significant decrease in one-time donations. This goes against our hypothesis that highlighting the match would increase donor conversion metrics by creating urgency and perceived value for donations.

In future experimentation, we should be cautious about assuming that all incentives will lead to positive outcomes. It is important to test different strategies and incentives to see what resonates best with our audience. Additionally, we should consider the impact of potential matching challenges on different donor segments and tailor our messaging accordingly. It is also crucial to closely monitor the results of experiments to quickly identify any negative impacts and make adjustments as needed.

experiments.title: How more concise and clear donation page copy impacts donor conversion metrics on a funnel
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that condensing the donation page copy for Pray for a Missionary visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because it reduces cognitive load and helps the users reach the call to action faster, while still preserving emotional and scriptural impact to keep them engaged.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/02/2025 - 08/11/2025
To improve donor conversion rates on IMB’s Pray for a Missionary funnel, we tested donation page copy that was more concise while still clearly communicating the value proposition of the offer. The treatment was shown to 50% of traffic, and the test ran for approximately five weeks.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment include:
**Concise copy is effective**: The results demonstrate that condensing donation page copy for the Pray for a Missionary funnel led to a significant increase in revenue. This suggests that minimizing cognitive load and allowing users to reach the call to action faster can positively impact donor conversion metrics.
 	**Emotional and scriptural impact is crucial**: Despite the copy being more concise, it was still able to preserve the emotional and scriptural impact necessary to keep users engaged. This indicates that maintaining the core value proposition and emotional connection with the cause is essential for donor conversion.
 	**Statistical significance**: The 100.0% statistical level of confidence in the results strengthens the validity of the findings. This high level of confidence indicates that the observed increase in revenue is likely a direct result of the condensed copy treatment.
**Continuing to test copy variations**: Explore more concise ways to communicate the value proposition and emotional impact to donors. Test different messaging strategies to see what resonates best with the target audience.
 	**Segmenting the audience**: Consider segmenting the audience based on donor behavior, demographics, or other relevant factors to personalize the donation page copy further. This personalized approach could further enhance donor conversion rates.
 	**Iterating and optimizing**: Regularly review and optimize donation page copy based on user feedback, analytics, and ongoing experimentation. Continuously testing and refining copy will help maintain and improve donor conversion metrics over time.

experiments.title: How defaulting donation form to recurring impacts recurring donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that defaulting the donation page for Bible Direct Ask donation page visitors to recurring will achieve an increase in recurring donations because it reduces decision fatigue and makes it feel like the expected choice, encouraging donors to follow through with the default option.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/11/2025 - 06/27/2025
In an effort to generate more recurring donors for IMB, we opted to default their Bible Direct Ask donation form to recurring transactions for 50% of traffic.
The only change made on the treatment was the form default.
This test ran for about 2 weeks.
We anticipated seeing some level of increase in recurring gifts, but our goal was to also not see a large drop off in overall transactions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are: Defaulting the donation page to recurring for Bible Direct Ask donation page visitors led to a significant increase in recurring donations by 647.9% at a valid 99.8% level of confidence. These results indicate that defaulting to recurring on a donation form will, in fact, lead to more recurring gifts on this funnel.

We did see a 14.5% decrease in overall transactions at a 53% LOC, but ultimately, a recurring donor is much more valuable than an episodic donor; therefore, the overall trade-off is worth the cost.

We kept the copy and value proposition consistent between the control and treatment to test how the form itself impacted recurring gifts, but in future experimentation, we plan to test using recurring language on the donation page along with a recurring defaulted form.

experiments.title: How anonymity on a landing page impacts email acquisition rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the option to send a note anonymously for Note of Encouragement landing page visitors will achieve an increase in email acquisition because it reduces the perceived pressure and lowers the barrier for participation.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/17/2025 - 06/30/2025
In an effort to increase the conversion rate on IMB’s Note of Encouragement funnel, we tested allowing users to mark their message as anonymous, allowing them to send their message without their name attached.
Our goal was that this treatment would allow visitors to feel more comfortable and confident submitting their encouraging notes, therefore leading to an increase in emails acquired on the page.
The treatment ran for about 2 weeks with half the traffic seeing the anonymous note check box.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the option to send a note anonymously on the Note of Encouragement landing page did not lead to an increase in email acquisition as hypothesized. In fact, the treatment resulted in a 19.3% decrease in email acquisition compared to the control group, with a high statistical level of confidence at 98.1%.

Moving forward, it is important to carefully consider the potential impact of changes to the user experience on conversion rates. While the idea of reducing perceived pressure and lowering barriers for participation seemed promising, the results show that anonymity may not always lead to increased engagement. These results indicate that the treatment added too much additional friction and not enough value to the visitors.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to thoroughly test and evaluate any proposed changes before fully implementing them. Additionally, conducting user testing and gathering feedback prior to launching an experiment can help uncover potential pitfalls and ensure that the changes align with the preferences and behaviors of the target audience.

Overall, this experiment highlights the importance of data-driven decision-making and the need to continually test and iterate on strategies to optimize conversion rates and user engagement.

experiments.title: How a Security Callout on a Landing Page Impacts Sign-Up Rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a security statement at the bottom of the form for 2025 Calendar landing page visitors will achieve an increase in email acquisition rate because it alleviates nerves about giving up personal information through our form.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/20/2025 - 02/24/2025
In an effort to boost our sign-up rate on IMB’s 2025 Calendar Landing Page, we decided to test into a security statement on the landing page.
The security statement read: “*We take data and privacy seriously. Your information will be kept safe and will be used to send you your calendar.”
Our hypothesis was that this statement would ease any nervousness of giving up personal information through our landing page form.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings:

1. The addition of a security statement at the bottom of the form on the 2025 Calendar landing page led to a significant decrease in email acquisition rates.

2. The decrease in email acquisition suggests that the security statement may have inadvertently raised concerns or doubts among visitors, leading them to reconsider providing their personal information.

Future Experimentation:

1. When testing security statements or trust signals on landing pages, it is crucial to carefully consider the phrasing and placement of such statements to avoid creating unintended negative effects.

2. In future experiments, it may be beneficial to test different variations of security statements or phrasing to determine what resonates best with visitors and effectively boosts email acquisition rates.

3. It is essential to continuously monitor and analyze the impact of any changes made to landing pages to ensure that they are having the intended positive effect on visitor behavior.

4. Collecting feedback from visitors through surveys or interviews can provide valuable insights into their perceptions of security statements and help in optimizing their effectiveness in future experiments.

experiments.title: How removing specificity from a direct ask donation page affects donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing specificity of the gift designation for Bible direct ask donors will achieve a decrease in donor conversion rate because donors may feel less emotionally connected or personally invested in a generalized ask, reducing their sense of urgency and clarity about the impact of their gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/22/2024 - 01/04/2025
The IMB is running a donation direct ask offer through paid Facebook advertising asking prospects to give to the IMB to fund Bibles being provided to different people groups around the world.
Due to issues of the gift designation, the IMB removed language about gift specificity to allow all donations to be designated where needed. Because of this, the “after” donation page did not have:
Gift handles specifying the donation amount
Specific language about what donating to this fund does
A goal thermometer
The results below show the conversion rates of donors during the “before the changes” time period compared to the “after the changes” time period.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that removing specificity of the gift designation for Bible direct ask donors led to a significant decrease in donor conversion rate. The "after the changes" group experienced a 92.6% decrease in donations compared to the "before the changes" group. This difference was statistically significant with a 100.0% level of confidence, indicating a clear impact of the changes made.

It is important to note, that our results from this direct ask offer went from the cheapest cost per donor ($206 GCPD) in the most expensive fundraising period ($17.33 CPM) to the most costly cost per donor ($384.17) in the least expensive fundraising period ($2.08). Meaning in the "before the changes" time period, it was costing the IMB $17.33 to show their ads to 1,000 people, receiving a 1.3% instant conversion rate, resulting in a $206 cost per donor. Whereas in the "after the changes" time period, it has cost the IMB only $2.08 to show their ads to 1,000 people. This should mean that our results are dramatically better! But with the new page, the conversion rate has tanked to 0.10%, resulting in an average cost per donor of $384.

In future experimentation, it is essential to consider the emotional connection and sense of urgency that donors feel when making a donation. Providing clarity about the impact of their gift and giving them a sense of ownership over where their donation is designated can lead to higher conversion rates. Therefore, it is important to communicate the specific goals and impact of the donation fund in order to motivate donors to contribute. Additionally, setting clear expectations and goals for fundraising campaigns, such as using a goal thermometer, can help create a sense of urgency and encourage donors to take action.

experiments.title: How adjusting a landing page form and image impacted the email acquisition rate

rows.name: Form Adjustments + New Layout + Image Left

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the form relocation and aligning the image to the left side for Prayer Calendar landing page visitors will achieve an increase in emails acquired because there will be less friction on the page and the visitor will perceive the form as less work since all content will be above the fold..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/09/2024 - 08/20/2024
In an effort to boost our email acquisition rate on IMB’s Prayer Calendar funnel, we decided to run an A/B/C split test with traffic equally split between each version.
Treatment 1 – relocated the landing page form the right hand side making the page look like a two column layout
Treatment 2 – relocated the landing page form the right hand side making the page look like a two column layout + making the image left aligned
Our hypothesis that the form relocation would make the landing page appear like a short experience for the user and the image adjustment seems small but the image is angled to face outward which we thought may have been adding unnecessary friction to the page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the form relocation and aligning the image to the left side of the Prayer Calendar landing page resulted in a significant increase in email acquisition rate. The treatment led to a 15.4% increase in email acquisition for all traffic with a statistical confidence level of 95.2% meaning that these results are valid and should be rolled out to all traffic.
This result indicates that the hypothesis was correct in assuming that these adjustments would reduce friction on the page and make it appear as a shorter and easier experience for the user. Moving forward, it is important to consider the impact of design elements on user experience and to continue testing and iterating on these factors to optimize conversion rates.
It is important to note that this was technically a multivariate test as we changed both the form location and used a condensed version between the control and treatment 1. This was unintentional, but it is clear that the combination of the form location, condensed fields, and left alignment of the image increased the number of emails acquired which was the final goal.
Future experimentation should focus on further refining the layout and design of the landing page to continue improving the user experience and email acquisition rates. It is also beneficial to involve user feedback and conduct usability testing to gain insights into how visitors interact with the page. By continuously testing and learning from these experiments, we can optimize our conversion funnels and improve overall performance.

experiments.title: How using an organic-looking post as a paid Facebook ad affects click-through rate and other downstream conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a Facebook ad that looks like an organic Facebook post for potential subscribers will achieve a higher CTR and lower CPC because it feels more human and not like an advertisement..
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 09/14/2023
This ministry client has campaigns that helps give relief to people around the world. We wanted to test into their Facebook ad click-through rate and see if we could get a higher down-stream conversion rate. IMB’s Facebook ads normally have designed graphics with a clear message, as you can see in Sample A below. The organic ad we created for this test has simple white font text on a navy background, it included no graphics unlike the control. We’ve tested running “organic posts” for other organizations and have seen a tremendous increase in click-through rate and a decrease in cost per click. We believe that the organic Facebook ad could create a higher click-through rate since it is simple and clear and looks like an organic Facebook post which humanizes the ad. This could also lead to a decrease in cost per click since the organic ad appearance does not seem as “markety” as the control graphic.
We tested this as an A/B test with a 50/50 split in targeting.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using a Facebook ad that resembles an organic post can significantly increase click-through rates and potentially decrease cost per click. The treatment ad, which had simple white font on a navy background and no graphics, outperformed the control ad with designed graphics and clear messaging.

The results of this experiment show a 51.3% increase in clicks for all traffic, with a high level of statistical confidence at 100.0%. This confirms the hypothesis that the organic-looking ad feels more human and less like an advertisement, leading to higher engagement from users.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing organic-looking ads against more traditional ad formats. This can help optimize ad performance and potentially reduce advertising costs. Additionally, considering the success of this experiment, it may be worth exploring the length of copy or color of organic ads.

experiments.title: How a more direct value proposition affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that direct ask ads for Facebook users will achieve an increase in conversion rate because they already understand the story of the situation and the needs.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/20/2023
IMB was raising money for missionaries in the Holy Land. It was a high-urgency event, and they didn’t know which way of presenting the value proposition would resonate best. They created four versions of the ads:
A story-based approach
A more aggressive version of #1
A needs-based approach
A direct ask
Though they hypothesized that the direct ask would work best, they launched these ads across all audiences to determine which approach was most impactful.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the direct ask approach for Facebook ads was the most impactful in terms of generating clicks and achieving a high conversion rate. Despite the hypothesis that the direct ask approach would work best, it was important to run the experiment and gather data to confirm this assumption.
Based on the results, it is clear that Facebook users already understand the story and needs, and they respond well to a direct ask for support. This suggests that in future experimentation, the nonprofit should continue using the direct ask approach in their ads, as it has shown to be effective in increasing conversion rates.
Additionally, this experiment highlights the importance of testing different approaches and not relying solely on assumptions or hypotheses. By running experiments and analyzing the results, the nonprofit can make data-informed decisions and optimize their ads for maximum impact.
Overall, the key takeaway from this experiment is that the direct ask approach for Facebook ads resonates well with the audience and should be used in future experimentation and campaigns.

experiments.title: How a monthly pop up during a donation process impacts recurring gifts
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that interrupting the giving experience for those who give one time gifts of $200 or less will achieve an increase in monthly gifts because we are giving the user an opportunity to read through the value proposition for a monthly gift before making a final decision .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/30/2023 - 04/16/2023
IMB has a multistep donation process within a widget. In an effort to increase monthly donations, we wanted to give people the opportunity to switch their one-time gift to a monthly gift. Our hypothesis is that if we give people the opportunity to switch their gift frequency before they submit their gift, we will see an increase in monthly gifts. The chart of standards used for this experiment is as follows:
If the one-time gift is $25 or less, the monthly ask is $5
If the one time gift is $26 – 50, the monthly ask is $10
If $50 – $100 , then $20 monthly ask
If $101 – $200 , then $25 monthly ask
If the gift is more than $200 then the pop up would not show up.
We ran this experiment on the main donation page which regularly receives a lot of organic traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that interrupting the giving experience for those who give one-time gifts of $200 or less with a pop-up prompting them to switch to monthly donations can potentially increase monthly donations. However, the results cannot be considered valid due to the statistical level of confidence being below 85%. This experiment resulted in a 25% increase in monthly donations with a level of confidence of 33%. We did not generate enough monthly gifts to validate the results of this experiment.

The donor conversion rate for all donations, however, did see a valid increase. It's unclear as to why this is the case considering that the pop up did not show up until the very last step of the giving process. The next iteration of this experiment will move the pop up after the first step of the giving experience as opposed to waiting until the end. That way, the interruption is done right after they have selected "one-time" as their gift frequency.

experiments.title: How adding clarity of a gift’s impact on a donation page affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that providing greater clarity to how a donor’s gift is used for IMB’s audience will achieve an increase in conversion rate because donors can better imagine the tangible impact of their gift. .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/12/2022 - 03/15/2023
IMB launched an advent ebook offer at the end of the calendar year. Given that this resource would have a short lifespan we wanted to maximize donor conversion rate in the funnel. We noticed that the existing donation page might read as vague or intangible to the reader. We hypothesized that by not simply talking about what IMB does, but how (and more importantly why a gift makes this possible) it would bring clarity to the IMB’s ask as well as what the donor’s gift will be used for—we believe these two changes together will lift donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The A/B experiment conducted by IMB showed that providing greater clarity to how a donor's gift is used can significantly increase conversion rates. The treatment group saw a 262% increase in donations for all traffic with a 99.9% level of confidence.
What's interesting is that both control and treatment say where the donors gift will go. But where the control seems to focus on this more as a way of reassuring the donor about allocation (and only gets as specific as physical and spiritual needs) the treatment go into detailed bullet points. Cross-cultural training, medical supplies, clean water—these are tangible benefits that we can imagine versus trying to define what a "physical" and "spiritual" need is.
It's likely that the addition of these bullet points decreases any type of anxiety that the prospective donor may have about how their donation will be used.

experiments.title: How a homepage takeover affects donor conversion rate at calendar year end
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that taking over the homepage with a donation form for main domain visitors` will achieve an increase in conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/30/2022 - 01/04/2023
IMB was running their calendar year end campaign around their Lottie Moon Christmas Offering. To reduce friction at a time of the year when conversion rate is highest, they wondered if turning their homepage into the donation page would increase donor conversion by reducing the necessary steps between the visit and a gift.
They set up a treatment where some donors would see a donation page with a link to “go to IMB.org” at the top of the page instead of the homepage.
To mitigate risk, they only included 25% of the traffic in this experiment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment, which showed a donation page on the first step, produced a 30.6% increase in conversion rate. This is significant, as experiments like this are often hard to validate at the end of the year, when motivation to give is highest. However, the reduction in friction showed that donors are still falling out of the funnel, even when coming in with high motivation.
This technique should not be overused, but could also be used with specific campaigns.

The IMB was running their calendar year end campaign, which was historically called the Lottie Moon Christmas Offering. They wondered how prevalent the brand awareness of Lottie Moon was, especially among younger donors, as opposed to the organizational brand.
They decided to test this hypothesis through a sticky bar that ran at the top of the website throughout the end of the year. The control gave the donor the offer to help the IMB, and the treatment gave them the offer to help missionaries through the LMCO.
They split traffic 50/50 to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:

experiments.title: How adding a transitional sentence in the header of the donation page impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a transitional sentence in the header of an instant donation page for ebook downloaders will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/28/2022 - 12/12/2022
The IMB has an advent offer where they ran a donation page experiment on the instant donation page in order to increase donor conversion rate. Typically instant donation pages only have a thank you statement or acknowledgement as the header but in order for the page to not seem like a confirmation page, we wanted to add a transitional sentence to let people know that there’s more to read and take action on in the donation page.
Traffic to this donation page was driven by both paid facebook ads and housefile email traffic
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results of this experiment indicate that IMB's audience do not respond to the transitional statement in the header. We saw a valid 74% decrease in donor conversion rate. This could be because in an effort to transition to the appeal quicker, we actually removed the "thank you" sentence originally in the control of this donation page. It could be that the act of thanking them first before asking them to take another action actually demotivated people to take the additional step to give.
A follow up test could be to include the thank you in the header and add the transitional sentence so that grateful sentiment isn't lost in the messaging.

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding project-specific value proposition language for prospective lottie moon donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/02/2022 - 08/27/2022
When looking at IMB’s high traffic areas in their “Give” section on their website , we saw most donations go through the “Give Now” button on the Lottie Moon callout on this particular landing page. We noticed that when you click through this particular button, you’re taken to a generic donation page with no value prop language our mention of Lottie Moon. We hypothesized that if we added value proposition language that was specific to Lottie Moon, there would be more congruency in the messaging and prospective donors would be more motivated to give when they read about the impact of their Lottie Moon gift. The control of this experiment had the generic donation page and the treatment added value proposition language centered around Lottie Moon.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment did not result in an increase like we had originally thought. We saw a valid 40% decrease in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 99%. While this was unexpected, we noticed that the highest negative impact was seen on desktop. On desktop this experiment is laid out as a 2 column experience. It could be that prospective donors had a difficult user experience with having to read the copy on the left hand side and having to scroll back up to make their gift on the right hand side of the page. Our hypothesis is that if we made the treatment a 1 column experience, we would see a lift in desktop.

IMB had a series of experiments done on their main donation page without much of a difference in results. Our continued hypothesis was that the individual comes to their main donation page with a high motivation to give and so there isn’t much that can be done on the main donation page to motivate the person to give. So, we wanted a way to capture the people who may have left the donation page without finishing the donation process. Our hypothesis was that an “abandoned donation” pop up would result an increase in donor conversion rate for their site visitors.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for nearly a month, this experiment resulted in a 24% valid lift in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 99%. This result affirms our hypothesis in that a pop up is effective in capturing those who may have abandoned the donation experience on their main donation page. Being reminded of the gift left behind and  the impact of the gift ("transform lives") will recapture those wouldn't have completed the donation process. It is recommended that this test is run again with a specific event attached to the pop up's donation button to discern what kind of lift we're seeing in donor conversion rate specifically through this pop up.

experiments.title: How focusing on the individual’s felt need affects email acquisition rate on an offer landing page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that focusing an individual’s felt need on a landing page will result in a higher email acquisition rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/19/2022 - 07/30/2022
IMB had an ebook called “Loving the Lost” that had a pre-existing landing page and funnel. When we relaunched this offer with an entirely new funnel we wanted to use the landing page copy of the previous funnel and test it against our new landing page. The new landing page was focused on the micro-problem, the individual’s felt need. Our hypothesis was that people would be more likely to fill out the form if they understood how this ebook is helpful to them.
The control for this experiment used the pre-existing landing page’s copy. The treatment had the new individual-focused language. Traffic to this funnel was primarily driven by paid Facebook ads.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment resulted in a valid 17% increase in email acquisition rate with a level of confidence of 98%. This means more people were motivated to download the ebook when they understood how this resource would personally help them and solve their problems: how can you love people that you do not know. The control was much more focused on the macro-problem of lostness and had a short invitation for the individual to join the IMB in prayer. This indicates that IMB's audience wants to know how they personally are affected by the larger problem at hand and focusing on the felt needs of the individual can increase the motivation to take action.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition and creating a one-column experience on a donation page impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a more robust value proposition on the main donation page and creating a more streamline experience for site visitors will increase donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/01/2022 - 06/25/2022
IMB had a main donate page that had no extensive value proposition language accompanying it. The only copy on the page was to “Advance the Kingdom” and that “100% of your gift goes to missionaries.” While these are aspects of their value proposition, we hypothesized that it did not fully encapsulate why someone should donate to the IMB. Using language from their value prop workshop, we pulled out several value statements to create a donation experience that outlines multiple reasons a person should give. We used 2 variants for this experiment: treatment 1 kept the same format but added more value proposition copy, and treatment 2 used the same value prop copy from treatment 1 but was a 1 column experience. While the page automatically becomes a 1 column experience in mobile, we wanted to see if there was a marked difference in donations on desktop through this treatment.
Traffic to this main donation page was driven by both organic and paid traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running for an extended period of time (21 days), we did not see a difference in results from either treatment. Treatment 1 resulted in a 12.6% decrease in donor conversion rate with a LoC of 82% while treatment 2 resulted in a 1.5% increase in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 12.3%– none of which validated. While it may seem like there was a decrease in revenue, this does not account for outlier gifts. When looking at the difference between desktop and mobile site visitors, we do not see a significant difference in results within those segments either.
There are follow up experiments that can be done on this page to further optimize the experience like: reducing the number of calls to action on the page, highlighting a "most popular" gift amount, or highlighting different value statements in the copy.
Upon further investigation, we noticed that most of the traffic going to this main donate page is actually from a "Lottie Moon" landing page--which can explain the disconnect in messaging that a site visitor may experience when going from a Lottie Moon page to this main donate page. Further testing around this funnel will be done in order to increase donor conversion rate.

experiments.title: How a past year-end donation page performed compared to the current year-end donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that running last year’s donation page copy versus this year’s donation page copy for CYE donation page visitors will yield no meaningful difference in overall donor conversion, because both pages communicate a sufficiently similar value proposition, urgency, and call to action, motivating giving at comparable levels.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/16/2025 - 12/19/2025
During IMB’s CYE Campaign, we observed unexpected softness in overall performance. While several potential contributing factors were identified, we prioritized validating whether the donation page itself was contributing to the issue.
To isolate that risk, we ran a direct A/B test comparing last year’s donation page copy to this year’s version. Although the test ran for only a few days, the results were directional and clear enough to warrant decisive action during IMB’s largest fundraising season.
This year’s donation page copy converted donors at a higher rate, allowing us to confidently remove last year’s version to protect conversions and revenue at a critical moment in the campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results indicate that the treatment donation page (last year’s copy) drove a 32.6% decrease in overall transactions (81% LOC). While the test did not reach full statistical validation, the directional signal was clear and unfavorable, reflecting a negative audience response to the treatment.

Given the strength of this directional learning—and the risk of suppressing conversion during IMB’s largest fundraising season—we ended the test.

These findings confirm that this year’s donation page copy is converting donors at a higher rate, indicating that the donation page itself is not the primary driver of the campaign’s softness. As a result, additional testing was required to better diagnose the underlying factors impacting performance.

experiments.title: How radically redesigning a year-end donation page impacts overall donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the condensed copy, countdown clock, and goal thermometer on the CYE Donation page will increase overall donor conversion by reducing friction in the giving experience and allowing donors—particularly mobile users—to complete the donation process more seamlessly.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/19/2025 - 12/25/2025
During IMB’s CYE campaign, we launched a three-way test designed to introduce meaningful variation and identify elements that could improve donor conversion.
Control: Standard CYE donation page
Treatment A: CYE donation page with countdown clock and goal thermometer
Treatment B: Hyper-condensed copy with countdown clock and goal thermometer
We anticipated Treatment B would outperform the other variations, as it significantly reduced page length—an advantage for mobile donors—while preserving urgency and social proof through the countdown clock and goal thermometer.
experiments.keyLearnings: The results show that Treatment A led to a modest 3.4% decrease in overall transactions (15.2% LOC), while Treatment B drove a substantial 27.8% decrease in transactions with a high level of directional confidence (90.1% LOC). Although neither variation reached full statistical validation, the magnitude and confidence of the decline associated with Treatment B clearly indicated a negative impact on donor conversion. Given the critical nature of the CYE fundraising window and the risk of continued conversion suppression, we have decided to remove Treatment B from rotation. This allowed Treatment A to continue running against the control, enabling additional learning without materially jeopardizing overall conversion or revenue during a high-stakes fundraising period.

experiments.title: Does Calling Out the Giving Tuesday Donor Goal in the Sticky Bar Increase Engagement?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the addition of the Giving Tuesday donor goal for IMB homepage visitors will increase engagement with the web asset, as making the goal visible taps into goal-oriented behavior. Visitors are more likely to click, explore, and give when they understand exactly what their participation contributes to.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/09/2025
During IMB’s 2025 Giving Tuesday campaign, we tested the addition of a donor goal to the homepage sticky bar. Our hypothesis was that clearly displaying the goal would increase engagement with the sticky bar—measured by higher click-through rates—and drive more motivated traffic further down the giving path.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Adding a donor goal to the homepage sticky bar resulted in a notable increase in engagement, yielding a 29.9% rise in clicks across all traffic. This suggests that prominently displaying a clear Giving Tuesday goal can attract more visitors to the giving pathway. However, because the confidence level fell below the standard 95% threshold, these engagement results should be interpreted as directional rather than definitive for future decision-making. When examining downstream giving behavior, both the control and treatment produced 9 total gifts.
Transactions per session: Treatment showed a 1.3% lift (2.1% LOC)
 	Transactions per click: Treatment showed a 22% decrease (43.5% LOC)
In other words, while the treatment succeeded in motivating more users to click into the donation experience, it did not increase their likelihood of completing a gift once they arrived at the donation page. The sticky bar drove more traffic to the page, but not more donors. Revenue: To prevent outliers from skewing insights, gifts over $500 were removed. Following this adjustment, the treatment resulted in a 93.7% decrease in revenue (94.1% LOC) compared to the control. This suggests that although engagement was higher, the additional traffic did not translate into higher-value giving behavior. In future experiments, it is essential to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test variations of the donor goal messaging to see if different phrasing or formats may have a more significant impact on user engagement. It is also important to continue monitoring and analyzing results from experiments to continually improve strategies for engaging with website visitors and driving them further down the giving path.

experiments.title: Does the fact that 100% of a donors gift goes to mission affect conversion rate?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding transparency about where the donor’s gift is allocated will increase the conversion rate because it builds trust and confidence in the organization by clearly showing how their generosity directly supports missionaries and Kingdom work.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/03/2024 - 12/03/2024
For Giving Tuesday, IMB wanted to test how adding transparency about the allocation of the donor’s gift would affect the donation conversion rate on both of their Giving Tuesday appeals.
We kept the control and treatment audiences consistent throughout both emails. The note of the allocation was added to the treatment emails as well as the donation page for those who received the treatment email.
They believed it would increase the conversion rate because it builds trust and confidence in the organization by showing how their gift directly supports missionary work.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding transparency about where the donor's gift is allocated can have a significant impact on the conversion rate for donations. In this case, the treatment group saw a 43.9% increase in donations compared to the control group, indicating that donors may be more inclined to give when they have a clear understanding of how their gift will be used.

Additionally, we calculated the impact of click-through rates in the emails and only saw a 1.1% increase with a 28.6% LoC. This means the addition of the transparency note did not have an effect on click-through rate, but helped increase giving once people got to the donation page.

To apply this learning in future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the impact of transparency on donation conversion rates. By conducting additional experiments with larger sample sizes and potentially different variations of allocation transparency, we can further validate the initial findings and determine the most effective strategies for communicating allocation to donors.

Overall, the results of this experiment suggest that transparency in donation appeals can build trust and confidence in the organization, ultimately leading to increased support for the mission.

experiments.title: How a sliding bar on a campaign donation page allowing donors to adjust their impact influences conversion rate and revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that allowing donors to select the level of their impact dynamically will increase donor conversion rate by increasing the perceived clarity and appeal of the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/09/2024
IMB is running a direct ask offer where people can give to fund Bibles, but we wanted to increase the tangibility and clarity of the donor’s impact on the campaign’s donation page. We hypothesized that allowing the donors to drag a slider that dynamically changes the number of people reached would increase the clarity of the offer when compared with the current experience.
We used VWO to create a graphical, interactive slider on the page that dynamically changed the gift amount and the corresponding number of people the gift would reach. We split traffic 50/50 and all campaign sources were directed to the test.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the dynamically changing slider for donors to select the level of their impact did not increase donor conversion rate as hypothesized. In fact, the treatment group experienced a 35.8% decrease in donations compared to the control group.

While the results are approaching validity, with a statistical level of confidence of 93.8%, further testing would be necessary to confirm these findings.

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the impact of any changes made to the donation page. It may be beneficial to conduct more qualitative research or user testing before implementing a new feature to ensure it resonates with donors and effectively communicates the impact of their contribution. Additionally, testing smaller changes or variations of the dynamically changing slider could help pinpoint what specifically led to the decrease in donations in this experiment.

experiments.title: How the addition of impact bullets on a direct ask donation page impacts donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding impact bullets to the donation page for Bible Direct Ask donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because the impact bullets give a more tangible connection for the donor’s impact of their gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/08/2024 - 09/10/2024
We are currently running a direct ask offer for IMB called God’s Word for the Nations which is a Bible Direct Ask. This offer asks individuals to donate so that IMB can fund the translation and distribution of Bibles in native languages across the world.
While this funnel was performing relatively well, we wanted to further optimize the process and lower our gross cost per donor.
Therefore, we tested the addition of impact bullets. These bullets notated how much impact a $5, $25, $50 gift would have in terms of number of Bibles.
$5 will provide 1 Bible
$25 will provide 5 Bibles
$50 will provide 10 Bibles
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding impact bullets to the donation page for the Bible Direct Ask offer resulted in a 15.1% increase in donations for all traffic. While this increase is substantial, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was only 75.8%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

Furthermore, we saw a 1.3% lift in revenue at a 52.2% LOC for the treatment. While neither of these metrics validated, they do suggest that IMB's audience resonated more with the impact bullets.

Moving forward, it is essential to continue testing the impact bullets or similar strategies in future experiments to determine if they consistently lead to increased donor conversions. Additionally, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to provide statistically reliable results before making any conclusions. By conducting further experiments with a larger sample size, we can validate the effectiveness of adding impact bullets and make more informed decisions about optimizing the donation process for the God's Word for the Nations offer.

experiments.title: How focusing on urgency in the final days of a high urgency campaign affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that focusing on urgency in the final days of a high urgency campaign for potential donors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because users will be reminded of the deadline and the importance of giving before then.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/27/2023 - 12/31/2023
The IMB was running their annual Lottie Moon Christmas Offering campaign. On the campaign donation page, they decided to test their standard value proposition which told the story of Lottie Moon against a page that increased urgency to give before the deadline of December 31st. This test ran on the final four emails of the campaign which led up to the deadline.
They split the traffic to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that focusing on urgency in the final days of a high urgency campaign may not necessarily lead to a higher donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment group, which received the urgency-focused messaging, experienced a decrease of 19% in transactions compared to the control group.

Based on these results, it is important to consider that urgency itself may not be a significant motivating factor for potential donors. Other factors such as the emotional appeal of the story or the credibility of the nonprofit may have a stronger influence on donor behavior.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to further explore the impact of urgency messaging on donor conversion rates. This could involve testing different variations of urgency messaging, such as emphasizing specific deadlines or highlighting the impact of giving before a certain date. Additionally, it could be valuable to investigate the potential interaction between urgency and other elements of the value proposition, such as emotional storytelling.

By conducting further testing and refining the messaging strategy, the nonprofit can gain a clearer understanding of what truly drives donor conversion and optimize their campaigns accordingly.

experiments.title: How a continuation headline impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a continuation headline for visitors will achieve an increase in our donor conversion rate because  it will provide clarity on their next steps and encourage them to continue reading down the page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/07/2023 - 01/05/2024
In an effort to increase our instant donor conversion rate on our Prayer Calendar funnel, we decided to run a continuation headline test. The new headline reads: “Your 18-month calendar will be mailed to your address shortly. Before you go, would you consider something?”
The continuation headline will help to encourage visitors to read further down the page, enabling them to read more value proposition, and then finally see the donation page.
By removing “Thank you” from the headline, we believe less people will perceive the donation page as the final step in the process and rather, make a donation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using a continuation headline on the Prayer Calendar funnel led to a significant increase in revenue for all traffic at a 73.8% lift. This result suggests that the continuation headline effectively communicated the next steps to the visitors and avoided the perception that their contribution was complete.
However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%. This means that while the results are promising, further testing is necessary to validate the findings and ensure that the continuation headline consistently generates positive results.
In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the continuation headline to gather more data and increase the statistical level of confidence. Additionally, it may be valuable to explore other variations in the headline to identify the most effective messaging for visitor conversion.
Overall, the findings of this experiment provide valuable insights for optimizing the Prayer Calendar funnel, but further testing and refinement are required to cement the effectiveness of the continuation headline.

experiments.title: How adding an anchor button on an experience page impacted conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding an anchor button at the top of the page for web traffic will achieve a higher conversion rate because it makes the offer more easily accessible.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/30/2023 - 11/22/2023
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
For November 2023, PTV released a brand-new children’s book by Julia Jeffress Sadler. To promote this page, PTV built an experience page with tons of graphics, testimonials from readers and images of the interior of the book. At the bottom of the page is where the web visitor can select the offer bundle they would like to receive with their generous donation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment was a decrease of 11.6% with a 92% level of confidence in transactions.
We believe that the version with the button actually caused web visitors to miss all value proposition for the offer and made it more transactional in nature.

experiments.title: How a Donation Interruptor Impacts Monthly Giving on an Offer’s Instant Donation Page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that donation interruptor  for users who pre-select a one-time gift will achieve an increase monthly donation conversion because psychologically it lowers the amount of their original gift, while providing value prop copy about how that smaller amount each month has a far greater impact..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/26/2023 - 07/02/2023
The Donation Interruptor is an experiment that we’ve tested on IMB’s general donation page in the past. For a greater collection of data, we wanted to test the experience on one of their offers, a Course on Prayer. The tabbed form on the donation page pre-selects One-Time, we hypothesized that a pop-up appearing directly after they submit their gift amount (but before they provide their payment info) will increase monthly gifts. The reason being that the popup will present the user value copy around how a lower amount (but each month) can make a greater impact even though a lower gift upfront.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that implementing the Donation Interruptor on the Course on Prayer offer resulted in a significant increase in monthly donations for all traffic. Although the statistical level of confidence is below the standard threshold of 95%, and the results are approaching validity.
This suggests that the interruptor pop-up, which highlights the impact of smaller monthly donations, has the potential to influence donors to choose the monthly giving option instead of a one-time gift. However, further testing is necessary to confirm these findings and ensure their validity.
One observation is that although this increased monthly gifts, the treatment does appear to lower the total number of gifts. This however, did not validate as a statistical result. It is something to continue observing, while also keeping in mind that repeated monthly gifts can still be an exponential revenue gain even despite a small decrease off in total gifts

experiments.title: How removing organizational language in an email appeal impacts revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing organizational language in an email appeal for prospective donors will achieve more revenue .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/09/2022
For Giving Tuesday, the IMB sent out email appeals to their housefile. In one of the emails we wanted to test how removing organizational language would impact either donor conversion rate or overall revenue. We hypothesized by replacing “the IMB” with words like “us” or “our,” the prospective donor would feel like they’re giving to people instead of a organization or corporate entity.
experiments.keyLearnings:
When pulling the results, the donor conversion rate was relatively the same. We saw a 16% increase in donor conversion rate but with only a level of confidence of 52%. We also saw little no impact in click-thru rate for these emails. However when looking at revenue data, we saw a 51% directional lift in revenue with a level of confidence of 87.8%. While this is not a statistically valid result, it can be surmised that there are some donors who may have been motivated to give a larger gift when reading the treatment copy. The treatment copy speaks to the donor as if they're joining "us" to accomplish something as opposed to giving to someone–making this much more communal in nature.
It's recommended we retest this concept and also include the donation page copy in the experiment so that the donation page copy also has the organizational language removed in the treatment experience.

experiments.title: Does including a specific giving handle in an email appeal lead to an increase in donor conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a specific giving handle for Email 2 recipients will achieve an increase in donor conversion  because  donors are more likely to give when they can quickly see what their generosity will accomplish.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/13/2026 - 01/13/2026
During IMB’s 2026 Winter Olympic campaign, we tested several approaches to increase donation page traffic and, ultimately, drive more gifts.
In Email 2, we tested a specific giving handle paired with a clear impact statement. The email highlighted a $50 gift and tied it directly to providing 50 booklets of the Gospel of John.
The goal of this test was to determine whether IMB’s housefile audience is more motivated to give when a clear, tangible value is attached to a specific donation amount.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that including a specific giving handle in Email 2 was directionally strong, resulting in a 50.1% increase in donations across all traffic. While this lift suggests meaningful donor responsiveness to clearly defined, impact-driven gift amounts, the test did not reach statistical validation. The confidence level of 63.0% falls below the standard 95% benchmark, indicating that the result should be interpreted as directional rather than conclusive.
Even without statistical validation, the outcome provides valuable insight into IMB’s housefile audience. The performance suggests that donors may be more motivated to give when a specific dollar amount is paired with a tangible ministry outcome, reinforcing the importance of clarity and impact in fundraising messaging.
Moving forward, continued testing will be essential to more clearly identify what drives donor conversion. Future experiments should be structured to reach a minimum statistical confidence threshold of 95% to ensure learnings can be acted on with greater certainty. Doing so will allow IMB to make more informed, data-backed decisions that strengthen donation page traffic, increase gift conversion, and ultimately grow the number of donors supporting IMB’s mission.

experiments.title: How the addition of a goal thermometer and countdown clock influence donor conversion during a high-urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the addition of a goal thermometer and countdown clock to the CYE Donation page will increase overall donor conversion, as these elements reinforce urgency and visible progress, helping donors feel both the timeliness and the collective impact of their gift at the moment of decision.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/25/2025 - 01/01/2026
At the tail end of IMB’s year-end campaign, we tested the isolated impact of adding a countdown clock and goal thermometer to the donation page. The test ran for approximately five days, with traffic evenly split between the two variants.
All copy and value proposition remained consistent across both pages; the only variables introduced were the countdown clock and goal thermometer. This approach enabled us to directly assess the impact of urgency and progress indicators on donor conversion, without confounding changes to messaging.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results indicate that adding a countdown clock and goal thermometer to the IMB year-end donation page had no meaningful impact on donor conversion. While the treatment showed a 2.7% decrease in conversion rate, the result carried a low level of confidence (24.2% LOC) and does not suggest a true negative effect.

Under different circumstances, this test would have benefited from a longer run time to achieve greater statistical clarity. However, given the limited window and high stakes of the year-end campaign, extending the test was not feasible. As a result, we conclude that urgency and progress indicators alone were not sufficient to materially influence donor behavior during this phase of the campaign.

experiments.title: How clarity about donor dollars on the homepage impacts engagement and downstream donor metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that transparent “100% of your gift” language for IMB homepage visitors will achieve an increase in engagement and downstream donor metrics because it establishes immediate trust in how donor dollars are used and creating a seamless message connection between the homepage and the giving page. because  it establishes immediate trust in how donor dollars are used and creating a seamless message connection between the homepage and the giving page.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/14/2025
To strengthen IMB’s homepage value proposition and increase clicks to the donation page, we tested adding a statement to the homepage graphic that clearly explains where donor dollars are used.
This message about how gifts are applied was also featured on the main giving page, creating stronger congruency between the microdecision to click “Donate” and the experience donors encounter once they arrive on the giving page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that using transparent language like "100% of your gift" did not yield the expected increase in engagement and downstream donor metrics. Despite the treatment version emphasizing how donor dollars are used, it actually resulted in a 13.2% decrease in clicks for all traffic compared to the control version.
Since the statistical level of confidence is only 68.3%, these results cannot be considered valid or reliable for future decision making. Therefore, it is important not to draw conclusions or make changes based on these results alone.
However, was the treatment more effective at motivating donors?
The short answer: No.
The treatment produced a 23.1% decrease in transactions at a 47% LOC including all transactions made.
However, in order to see the most accurate results for this test, we removed all gifts over $500, and saw the treatment actually has a 60% decrease in overall transactions at a valid 95.1% LOC.
In future experimentation, it is essential to consider factors such as the language used, the placement of the message, and the overall messaging strategy. It may be beneficial to further test different variations of the message or to explore other elements of the homepage that could impact engagement and donor behavior. Additionally, ensuring a higher level of statistical confidence in the results before making significant changes is crucial to making informed decisions.

experiments.title: How does transparency in communicating gift allocation affect donor conversion performance?
experiments.researchQuestion: Including explicit language about gift allocation on the Deaflympics donation page will increase donor conversion metrics, as transparency builds trust and reassures donors that their gifts directly support the mission, rather than administrative costs.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/09/2025 - 10/05/2025
To improve donor conversion on IMB’s Deaflympics donation page, we introduced more direct, transparent language about how each gift is used.
While we’ve often said, “100% of your gift goes directly to missionaries on the field,” this version takes it a step further—explicitly clarifying that no portion of these funds is used for administrative or overhead expenses.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key takeaway from this experiment is that including explicit language about gift allocation on the Deaflympics donation page resulted in a 15.7% increase in transactions across all traffic. This suggests that transparency regarding how donations are utilized can positively impact donor conversion metrics by fostering trust and ensuring that donors know their gifts directly support the mission. However, the statistical level of confidence for this result was only 45.5%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for making future decisions. It is also important to note that the treatment received a $60,000 gift, which significantly skewed our revenue metric. In future experimentation, it is essential to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to generate statistically significant results.

Additionally, considering factors that may have influenced the outcomes, such as external events or changes in donor behavior, can help in designing more robust experiments. Overall, the concept of transparency in communicating how donations are used is promising for improving donor conversion rates. Future experiments should continue to explore this idea while also focusing on obtaining valid and reliable results to inform decision-making effectively.

experiments.title: How adding a signature block with image in a direct appeal to the housefile impacts email clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including the email signature block for housefile appeal receiver’s will achieve an increase in engagement with the email because it adds a personal touch—allowing readers to put a face to the name and therefore connect more with the ask.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/05/2025 - 08/05/2025
IMB has been working to optimize the email templates used to communicate with their housefile, with the goal of adding more personal touches to make each message feel like it’s coming from an individual rather than the organization as a whole. To support this effort, we tested adding a signature block with the sender’s photo to a direct appeal email. The control group received our standard typed sign-off, while the treatment group received the signature block with the image.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the treatment group, which received the signature block with the sender's image, experienced a decrease in Clicks for All Traffic compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 73.4%, which is below the recommended threshold of 95%. Therefore, these results cannot be considered reliable for making future decisions.

In future experimentation, it is crucial to ensure that the sample size is adequate to provide statistically significant results. Additionally, it is essential to carefully monitor and analyze the data to determine the impact of any changes made to the email templates. While personalizing emails with a signature block may seem like a good idea intuitively, it is important to test this hypothesis rigorously to validate its effectiveness before implementing it on a larger scale. Further experimentation with different variations of personalization elements in emails could help determine the most effective strategies for increasing engagement with the audience.

experiments.title: How adding a signature block with image in a direct appeal to the housefile impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including the email signature block for housefile appeal receivers will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because it adds a personal touch—allowing readers to put a face to the name and therefore connect more with the ask.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/13/2025
IMB has been working to optimize the email templates used to communicate with their housefile, with the goal of adding more personal touches to make each message feel like it’s coming from an individual rather than the organization as a whole. To support this effort, we tested adding a signature block with the sender’s photo to a direct appeal email. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 58.6%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%. This means that the results are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.
