experiments.title: How a two column layout impacts downstream donor metrics on a direct ask email to the housefile
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a two column approach for iMB housefile contacts will achieve an increase in downstream donor metrics because it offers an additional CTA to give.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/29/2025
For IMB’s Ghana Training Direct Ask offer, we tested our standard one-column email format vs a two-column format that featured a couple of calls-to-action.
On desktop, the treatment was featured as two columns at about 70/30 between the main content and the additional CTAs. However, on desktop, the “second” column went below the main content of the email. Therefore, it will be imperative to look at gifts based on device type to fully understand the impact of the treatment variation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the two-column format with additional calls-to-action in the email for IMB's Ghana Training Direct Ask offer resulted in a 60.0% increase in donations for all traffic. This indicates that providing an extra CTA to give can positively impact donor engagement and conversion rates.

However, the statistical level of confidence for this result was only 59.5%, which falls below the generally accepted threshold of 95% for valid results. Therefore, while the experiment showed a positive outcome, the lack of high statistical confidence means that these results should not be solely relied upon for decision making.

These results indicate that having a secondary CTA to give on desktop did in fact increase transactions, but had no impact on mobile.

In future experimentation, it is important to continue testing different layouts and CTAs in emails to optimize donor engagement. It would also be interesting to test our standard layout against a double CTA within a direct ask email to see if it was truly the secondary column or simply the secondary CTA to give.

experiments.title: How connecting an offer’s impact to the organizations mission impact email acquisition on a funnel landing page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that “Connecting an offer’s impact to the organization’s mission for Note of Encouragement landing page visitors will achieve an increase in email acquistion because it reinforces the visitor’s sense of purpose and alignment with the organization’s values, increasing the likelihood of engagement and boosting email acquisition.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/16/2025
To boost email acquisition rates on IMB’s Note of Encouragement funnel, we tested new landing page copy that connected the user’s action to IMB’s mission and the Great Commission. The goal was to present the act as a meaningful partnership in the gospel, rather than just a kind gesture, with the expectation that this would drive higher email acquisition rates.
By clearly linking how a simple note supports missionaries and aligns with IMB’s mission, this version gives users a stronger reason to engage and complete the form.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that connecting an offer's impact to the organization's mission on the Note of Encouragement landing page did not lead to an increase in email acquisition rates. Despite the hypothesis that reinforcing the visitor's sense of purpose and alignment with the organization's values would boost engagement, the treatment actually saw a 2% decrease in Emails Acquired for All Traffic.
 	It is crucial to note that the statistical level of confidence was only 36.0%, well below the 95% threshold needed for results to be considered valid. Therefore, these results should not be used for future decision making.
 	In future experimentation, it is important to continue testing different strategies and messaging to determine what resonates best with users. It may be beneficial to explore other ways of tying the visitor's action to the organization's mission and values to see if a different approach yields more positive results. Additionally, it is essential to ensure that future experiments have a higher statistical level of confidence to draw valid conclusions.

experiments.title: How reframing funnel landing page copy as a gospel partnership impacts email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reframing the funnel landing page copy for “Note of Encouragement” visitors will achieve an increase in email acquisition because it shows how a simple note strengthens the mission of IMB therefore giving the visitor a stronger reason to engage.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/27/2025 - 06/16/2025
In an effort to boost our email acquisition rate on IMB’s Note of Encouragement funnel, we tested into landing page copy that would frame the act as a way to partner in the gospel versus a transactional experience. We wanted their audience to feel like they were truly playing a part in the mission of IMB.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the treatment, which reframed the landing page copy for "Note of Encouragement" visitors to emphasize partnering in the gospel, resulted in a 2.2% decrease in email acquisition for all traffic compared to the control. However, the statistical confidence level of 40.0% is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%, making the results inconclusive and unreliable.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to generate statistically significant results. Additionally, it may be beneficial to further refine the messaging and framing of the landing page copy to effectively communicate the value proposition to visitors and encourage email acquisition. A/B testing remains a valuable tool for optimizing conversion rates, but it is crucial to interpret results with caution and rely on statistically significant findings for decision making.

experiments.title: How an adding a relevant image to a direct ask donation page impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an image of the anonymous missionaries for Anonymous Missionaries Direct Ask Donation page will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because the image provides visual representation as to what an anonymous missionary is—therefore, helping visitors grasp the idea more quickly and evoking a stronger emotional connection.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/12/2025 - 03/03/2025
To improve donor conversion rates on IMB’s Anonymous Missionary Direct Ask, we tested the impact of adding a relevant image to the donation page.
This specific offer can be challenging to communicate effectively, so our hypothesis was that an image depicting “anonymous missionaries” would provide greater clarity to visitors, ultimately leading to higher donor engagement and conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding an image of anonymous missionaries to the donation page for Anonymous Missionaries Direct Ask did not lead to the expected increase in donor conversion metrics. In fact, the treatment group saw a significant decrease in donations compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence was below the generally accepted threshold of 95%, meaning that these results cannot be considered valid, but the results are trending in a negative direction.
Due to these results, we looked further into the where the traffic came from to see if it performed better or worse on different types of devices.
Sure enough - mobile users saw an almost valid 71.8% decrease in transactions (94.5% LOC). This indicates that the image negatively impacted potential donors who visited the donation page on their mobile device.  This could be due to the fact that image was somewhat larger and required more scrolling on mobile that desktop.
Desktop users saw an invalid 114.9% lift in transactions at a 47.4% LOC. In future testing, it may be beneficial to only test large images like this on desktop to minimize the amount of friction on the page for mobile users.
In future experimentation, it is important to carefully analyze the results and consider the statistical significance before drawing conclusions. It is also crucial to ensure that the hypothesis is well-grounded and based on solid evidence. Further testing may be needed to determine the true impact of adding an image to the donation page for this specific campaign. Additionally, it could be beneficial to explore other potential factors that may influence donor conversion rates, such as messaging, layout, or incentives.

experiments.title: How a humanized sticky bar on a high-urgency campaign donation page impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the presence of the humanized sticky bar on the CYE Donation page for email 16 visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because the personalized message boosts trust and creates a more personalized experience for the potential donor.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/30/2024 - 12/30/2024
During IMB’s CYE campaign, we implemented a humanized sticky bar on the donation page as part of the strategy for Email 16.
This sticky bar ran for a full day, with the test limited to users who accessed the donation page via a link embedded in Email 16.
The sticky bar treatment was shown to 50% of the page’s traffic, allowing us to evaluate its effectiveness in driving engagement and conversions in this specific segment of our audience.
The control featured no sticky bar.
The treatment sticky bar featured Chuck Anderson, sender of Email 16, and had the following copy: “There’s only one day remaining to support IMB missionaries through your year-end gift to the Lottie Moon Christmas Offering®. As a reminder, your gift will play a crucial role in sharing the joy and peace of Jesus with those who have yet to hear His name. – Chuck Anderson”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the humanized sticky bar featuring Chuck Anderson on the donation page for Email 16 visitors did not result in an increase in donor conversion metrics. In fact, the treatment saw a 15.0% decrease in transactions for all traffic with a statistical confidence level of 25.6%, indicating that the control group performed better.
It is important to note that the statistical confidence level is below the threshold of 95%, which means that these results are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.
In future experimentation, it is important to consider the impact of humanized elements like sticky bars on donor behavior. It may be beneficial to further test different variations of humanized elements or personalize them based on donor preferences to see if this can lead to an increase in conversions. Additionally, ensuring a longer experiment duration and a larger sample size can help in obtaining more reliable and valid results for future decision making.

experiments.title: How yes/no “autonomy” language on a pop-up impacts conversion rate during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a yes and no option for homepage visitors during CYE will achieve an increase in the donor conversion rate because the autonomy to make a micro-decision allows the visitors to feel in control before being asked to make a gift.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/06/2025
For IMB’s CYE campaign, we decided to test into donor autonomy on the homepage pop-up. We have had success with this concept with other clients, so we anticipated this concept would also perform well for IMB.
The hypothesis for this test is that having an option to say “yes” or “no” gives the potential donor a micro-decision to feel as if they are in control of the situation.
The control featured a single CTA: “Make my gift now »”
The treatment offered two options: “Yes, I’ll make a gift” and “No, not at this time” (this option closed out the pop-up)
We ran the pop-up from Dec 9 – Dec 27, 2025 with 50% of traffic receiving the treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that providing visitors with the autonomy to make a micro-decision on the homepage pop-up can lead to an increase in donor conversion rate. The treatment, which included a "Yes, I'll make a gift" and "No, not at this time" option, resulted in a 25.0% increase in donations for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only at 82.7%, falling below the generally accepted threshold of 85% for validity.

Despite the statistical level of confidence falling short, the experiment still provides valuable insights. In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue exploring the concept of donor autonomy and how it influences visitor behavior. Additionally, it may be helpful to further refine the language and options presented in the pop-up to see if different variations can yield even better results.

Overall, the learnings from this experiment can inform future testing strategies and emphasize the importance of giving visitors a sense of control in their decision-making process on the website. This insight can be applied to optimize donation processes and potentially improve conversion rates in future campaigns.

experiments.title: How adding a signer’s image in an email footer signature in a high urgency appeal impacts clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including Chitwood’s image in the email footer for CYE Email 12 will achieve an increase in clicks because the image will enhance the personal connection between the organization and the reader and humanize the message. because  the image will enhance the personal connection between the organization and the reader and humanize the message.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/06/2025
In an effort to drive higher email engagement during IMB’s CYE campaign, we conducted a test incorporating an image of Paul Chitwood, IMB’s President, in the email sign-off. The hypothesis was that featuring Chitwood’s image would enhance the personal connection with readers, allowing them to associate a face with the name and fostering a stronger sense of trust and rapport. This, in turn, was expected to increase the likelihood of readers making a donation.
We implemented this test in Email 12 of the CYE campaign, with 50% of the traffic receiving the version featuring Chitwood’s image to assess its impact on engagement and conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that including Paul Chitwood's image in the email footer for CYE Email 12 did not result in the expected increase in clicks. In fact, the treatment group experienced a 3.8% decrease in Clicks for All Traffic compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 64.7%, which is below the recommended threshold of 95%.
Based on these results, it is clear that the impact of including Chitwood's image on email engagement is inconclusive. Therefore, it is not advisable to make any definitive conclusions or decisions based on this experiment alone. In future experimentation, it would be important to conduct further tests with a larger sample size and ensure that the results reach a higher level of statistical confidence before implementing any changes to the email strategy.
Furthermore, we saw a 24.5% decrease in transactions at a 61.4% LOC. While this metric did not reach 95% validity, it does suggest that the presence of Chitwood's image reduced the motivation to give once those who clicked landed on the donation page.
Finally, it may be beneficial to explore other ways to enhance personal connections and trust with readers, such as through compelling storytelling, personalized content, or highlighting the impact of donations. By continuously testing and refining different strategies, IMB can better understand what resonates with their audience and optimize their email campaigns for improved engagement and conversions.

experiments.title: How gift array options in a high urgency sticky bar impact donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding the gift array to IMB’s CYE sticky bar for homepage visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because the gift array minimizes the number of steps that need to take place on the actual donation page therefore minimizing friction and decision making resulting in more gifts.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/06/2025
In an effort to boost IMB’s conversion rate from their CYE Sticky Bar, we opted to test into including a gift array on the sticky bar.
The gift array options were fully functional, and when clicked would auto fill the donation page with the applicable amount selected. We had three gift array options available on the sticky bar: $25, $75, and $250.
This test ran from Dec. 11 – Dec. 27th, 2024 with 50% of the traffic seeing the treatment version (gift array).
We hypothesized that adding the gift array would reduce friction on the donation page by minimizing the steps needed to complete a donation, ultimately resulting in more gifts.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the addition of a gift array on the CYE sticky bar for homepage visitors did not result in an increase in donor conversions for IMB. In fact, the treatment version of the sticky bar with the gift array saw a 23.4% decrease in donations compared to the control group.
Despite the decrease in donations, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was only at 66.5%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%. This means that the results of this experiment are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.
In terms of overall revenue, the control produced $15,686 and the treatment produced $10,930. Resulting in a 30.3% decrease in revenue at a 74.8% LOC.
We also looked further into the gift array amounts to see if one greatly outperformed the others.
$25 Button5 gifts$120 in Revenue$75 Button2 gifts$200 in Revenue$250 Button5 gifts$1,500 in Revenue
The remaining gifts came through the "More options" button that did not preselect a gift amount in the form meaning about half of users chose the "more options" button.
While these results are inconclusive, they do suggest that iMB's audience may react more positively to a standard sticky bar rather the gift array options.
In future experimentation, it would be important to conduct further testing with a larger sample size and potentially tweak the gift array options or placement on the sticky bar to see if different configurations could yield more positive results. It is also crucial to ensure that statistical significance is reached before making any conclusions or implementing changes based on A/B test results.

experiments.title: How including compelling images in a high urgency appeal impacts clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including compelling images in a high urgency appeal for CYE Email 14 will achieve an increase in clicks because the presence of the images allows the reader to visually connect withe the mission of the IMB.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/06/2025
To increase email click rates during IMB’s CYE campaign, we tested the impact of adding compelling images to Email 14. The treatment was presented to 50% of the recipients of this email.
Our hypothesis was that incorporating these visuals would help readers establish a stronger emotional connection with IMB’s mission, thereby increasing the likelihood of clicking the donation link.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that including compelling images in high urgency appeal emails may not necessarily lead to an increase in clicks for donation links. The treatment group, which received emails with images, actually saw a 2.9% decrease in clicks compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 51.5%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%.

Therefore, these results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making. In future experimentation, it would be important to thoroughly analyze the data and ensure that the findings are statistically significant before drawing any conclusions. It may also be beneficial to test different types of images or messaging to see what truly resonates with recipients and drives them to click on donation links.

experiments.title: How adding transparency about the allocation of a donor’s gift affects conversion rates in an email appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding transparency about where the donor’s gift is allocated will increase the conversion rate because it builds trust and confidence in the organization by clearly showing how their generosity directly supports missionaries and Kingdom work.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/16/2024
IMB tested how adding transparency about the allocation of the donor’s gift would affect the donation conversion rate on both of their Giving Tuesday appeals. We kept the control and treatment audiences consistent throughout both emails. The note of the allocation was added to the treatment emails as well as the donation page for those who received the treatment email.
This experiment saw a 43.9% lift in donor conversion rate with a 90% LoC.
Because it did not validate, they wanted to test it again on one of their CYE email appeals. The set-up was the exact same as the GT appeals.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment include:
1. Adding transparency about the allocation of a donor's gift can have a significant positive impact on the conversion rate.
2. The hypothesis that transparency builds trust and confidence in the organization, leading to increased donations, was supported by the results.
3. Despite the treatment showing a substantial increase in donations, the statistical level of confidence fell below the threshold of 85%, making the results inconclusive.
4. These results should not be used for future decision-making due to the lack of statistical validity.
In future experimentation, it is important to:
1. Continue testing the impact of adding transparency about allocation on donation conversion rates to validate the findings.
2. Ensure that experiments are properly powered and have a sufficient sample size to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence.
3. Consider other factors that may influence donation behavior to develop a more comprehensive understanding of donor motivations.
4. Use the learnings from this experiment as a basis for formulating new hypotheses and designing future experiments to optimize donation conversion rates effectively.

experiments.title: How including giving handles on a donation page during a recurring giving campaign impacts donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including giving handles  for the Recurring Giving donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because the gift’s impact will be more tangible for the donor. because  the gift’s impact will be more tangible for the donor.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/24/2024 - 11/04/2024
During IMB’s Fall Recurring Giving Campaign, we decided to test including giving handles within the donation page copy.
Both the control and the treatment had the same gift array and the gift array aligned with the amounts within the giving handles. These giving handles ranged from $5 – $75.
This donation page did default to monthly, but one-time giving was still an option.
It is worth noting that this campaign was shut down early, so this test did not get enough traffic to validate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that including giving handles in the donation page copy led to a 29.3% decrease in Revenue for All Traffic with a 26.4% statistical level of confidence and a 10.1% decrease in Transactions for All Traffic at a 28.3% level of confidence. While neither of these results are trending in the direction we intended, we did see a 210.8% lift in recurring donations at a 93.4% level of confidence.
The recurring donation metrics was extremely close to validating which does suggest that the giving handles resonated well with monthly givers. It is worth testing further into how giving handles can compel IMB's donor base to give.
In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that there is enough traffic to validate the results. Additionally, it may be beneficial to further test the impact of including giving handles with different messaging or positioning to see if there is a way to communicate the impact of donations effectively without negatively impacting conversion metrics. It is also crucial to consider the potential effects of messaging on donor behavior and to continuously iterate and test different approaches to optimize donation page performance.

experiments.title: How a dynamic homepage donate button on mobile impacts clicks and downstream donor metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the dynamic text on a donate sticky footer for IMB homepage visitors will achieve an increase in button clicks and downstream revenue metrics because the dynamic, typing text is more eye catching to the user .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 09/10/2024
In an effort to push more of IMB’s homepage traffic to their main donation page, we decided to run a test of adding an additional “give now” button to the homepage.
Control: No changes
Treatment 1: Additional Give Now button sticky footer (“Give Now”)
Treatment 2: Additional Give Now button sticky footer (“Give Now”) + Dynamic Text (Types out “Send the Gospel”)
Our hypothesis was that the dynamic text would increase the number of clicks to the donation page because it was more eye catching than Treatment 1.
This test only ran on mobile devices.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a donate sticky footer on the IMB homepage resulted in a 42.3% increase in donations for all traffic. Whereas, the dynamic donate sticky footer actually resulted in a 13.5% decrease in donations at 34% LOC.

While neither of these metrics validated, it does suggest that the static donate sticky footer was helpful in pushing more of IMB's homepage traffic to the donation page.

We can also look in clicks based on each treatment.

Sticky Footer: 104 Button Clicks

Sticky Footer + Dynamic Text: 88 Button Clicks

This data suggests that the dynamic, typing text was not as beneficial as the static text in directing visitors to the donation page.

Due to these results, we have chosen to roll out the static button to all mobile visitors.

experiments.title: How adding project-specific value proposition language to a donation page affects donor conversion rate (Pt. 2)
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding value proposition language to a donation page and making it a single column experience for prospective lottie moon donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/01/2022 - 10/13/2022
When looking at IMB’s high traffic areas in their “Give” section on their website , we saw most donations go through the “Give Now” button on the Lottie Moon callout on this particular landing page. We noticed that when you click through this particular button, you’re taken to a generic donation page with no value prop language our mention of Lottie Moon. We hypothesized that if we added value proposition language that was specific to Lottie Moon, there would be more congruency in the messaging and prospective donors would be more motivated to give when they read about the impact of their Lottie Moon gift.
This is the second iteration of this experiment because we noticed that desktop experienced a large decrease in conversions. So, we hypothesized that we could use the same language but change the layout of the page so that it was a single-column experience so that the user could read through all the value prop language before making a decision
experiments.keyLearnings: Our first results showed a valid decrease in donor conversion rate. However our secondary hypothesis did not prove to be true. This experiment resulted in a 7% decrease in donor conversion rate with only a level of confidence of 46% - meaning there is not statistical difference in the treatment. Upon closer examination it seems as if the donation form inadvertently got changed from the control's donation form. The header of the form changed and the gift designation selection was removed. These additional variables may have also impacted the results of our experiment.

experiments.title: How pulling the home donate button out of the hamburger menu on mobile impacts clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that pulling the mobile donate button out of the hamburger menu for mobile homepage visitors will achieve an increase in clicks on the donate button because the button will be more visible and accessible therefore reducing friction for mobile users. .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/17/2024 - 08/01/2024
We have seen a positive impact with other clients pulling their home donate button on mobile out of the hamburger menu to make it more visible to the visitor, so we wanted to test this on IMB’s homepage.
This test ran only on mobile with traffic equally split between the control and the treatment.
The treatment had a “Give Now” button pulled out of the hamburger menu, but we also left it within the hamburger menu to view clicks on both.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that pulling the mobile donate button out of the hamburger menu for the IMB homepage led to a 32.2% increase in clicks just on the donate button within the hamburger menu.

As well as, the new mobile donate button resulted in a total of 78 clicks. While the control button within the hamburger menu only had 22.

While this test is difficult to validate, it does not suggest that having the donate button more visible to the user on mobile devices is helpful in getting more users to the main donation page.

**Note: the 'Give Now - Mobile Header' button is tracking clicks on the NEW donate button that appeared outside of the hamburger menu. The 'Give Now - Mobile Menu' button is tracking clicks for both the control and treatment of the original donate button within in the hamburger menu.

experiments.title: How condensing a form and using more positive copy for a content offer impacts the email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that condensing the landing page form and using more positive copy for prayer calendar landing page visitors will achieve an increase in emails acquired because the form will be perceived as less questions to fill out and the positive tone will resonate more with the visitor.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/02/2024 - 08/07/2024
In an effort to boost our email acquisition rate on IMB’s main Prayer Calendar funnel, we decided to run a 3-way test. Treatment 1 condensed the landing page form so that it would be perceived as shorter for the user, and treatment 2 also condensed the form as well as used a more positive tone in the copy by changing the first line of the body copy.
Our hypothesis was that both of these changes would increase the email acquisition rate because it would reduce friction on the landing page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that both condensing the landing page form and using a more positive tone in the copy had little to no impact on the email acquisition rate for the Prayer Calendar landing page.

While these were not the results we were anticipating, it is interesting that the more positive copy did not have more of an impact on the landing page visitor.

Although we did not get any valid metrics from this test, it does tell us that the long form on the landing page does not negatively impact the visitor's motivation to complete the landing page.

We plan to continually run experiments on this funnel and test the motivations of IMB's audience.

experiments.title: How using a shorter more concise landing page for email prospecting traffic impacts the email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using shorter and more concise copy for Pray for a Missionary landing page visitors acquired through email prospecting will achieve an increase in the email acquisition rate because it would reduce friction on the page by getting to the purpose much quicker.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/06/2024 - 08/07/2024
In an effort to build a funnel that resonated best with the traffic IMB was receiving from email prospecting efforts, we decided to test using a shorter and more concise landing page solely for individuals who received our Pray for a Missionary prospecting emails.
We used VWO audience segmentation to be sure that this test did not impact normal traffic to the landing page.
Our hypothesis was that using more concise copy would help visitors understand the purpose of the page quicker and therefore reduce friction on the page. We tend to think traffic coming in through prospecting channels want quick and easy information to consume so we wanted to put that concept to the test.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that using shorter and more concise copy on the Pray for a Missionary landing page for visitors acquired through email prospecting resulted in a 2.5% increase in emails acquired for all traffic. While this is a positive outcome, the statistical level of confidence was only 51.5%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95% for validity, meaning that these results were ultimately flat.

Therefore we can not make any decisions based on this test. It would be interesting to test whether additional copy and value proposition for email prospecting traffic has a positive impact on email acquisition since our hypothesis was not proven with this test.

experiments.title: How using a campaign subject’s story to increase relevancy will affect donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that telling the story of Lottie Moon for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in conversion rate because it will give relevancy to the title of the campaign for people who might not be aware.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/18/2023 - 12/27/2023
The IMB was running their annual Lottie Moon Christmas Offering campaign. They knew that they had been growing their new donor file over the past few years, and were curious how they should position “Lottie Moon” (who was a former missionary). On the campaign donation page, they decided to test their standard value proposition against one that told the story of Lottie Moon, in order to give more relevancy to the name of the campaign.
The control page identified the need, and the treatment page prefaced the need with the story of Lottie Moon. They split the traffic to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that telling the story of Lottie Moon on the donation page resulted in an increase in conversions. The treatment group, which received the Lottie Moon story, had a 27.2% higher conversion rate compared to the control group.

However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence for this result was only 76.5%, which is below the recommended threshold of 85%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to collect more data and run the experiment for a longer period of time to increase the statistical level of confidence. Additionally, it may be helpful to make adjustments to the experiment design or implementation to minimize any potential confounding factors or biases.

Overall, while the initial results are promising, it is necessary to conduct further testing to fully validate the impact of telling the story of Lottie Moon on the donation page.

experiments.title: How adding clarity to a donation page for Giving Tuesday affects donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that providing clarity on the donation page will increase donor conversion rate because people have a clear understanding to what their gift is doing.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/04/2023
For Giving Tuesday this year, we wanted to test additional clarity on the donation page to see how IMB’s donor base would react.
The control offers an emotional appeal that focuses on the history of the Lottie Moon Christmas Offering and emphasizes the tradition of the offering. On the other hand, the treatment page focuses on a time sensitive appeal by mentioning Giving Tuesday specifically, and lays out a more straightforward and urgent need for financial support.
We believe that the added clarity will allow for greater comprehension of the donation page leading to an increase in performance.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that providing clarity on the donation page did not result in an increase in donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment page experienced a decrease in donations compared to the control page with a drop of 6.3% with 35% LOC.

Based on this outcome, it is necessary to retest this theory before implementing it. However, the current results suggest that IMB's audience does not respond positively to urgent time-bound appeals, but rather they prefer the emotional appeal and tradition of the Lottie Moon Christmas Offering, as it seems to resonate more with the donor base.

In future experiments, it would be beneficial to explore other factors that could potentially increase donor conversion rate, such as different messaging or design elements on the donation page. It is also important to ensure that the statistical level of confidence is above 95% in order to make valid conclusions from the experiment results.

experiments.title: How using an organic-looking post as a paid Facebook ad with a brighter color background affects click-through rate and other downstream conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a brighter color background for the Facebook ad instead of a darker color for Facebook users will achieve an increase in click through rate and decrease in cost per click because the brighter color background will create a sense of vibrancy and attention-grabbing effect, capturing users’ focus and prompting them to take action.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 09/14/2023
We have recently tested Facebook ads for this ministry client, which was an experiment between organic vs. normal graphic ads. We found that the organic post led to a higher click-through rate and donor conversion. Knowing that, we then wanted to conduct an A/B test between two different organic Facebook ads. The control Facebook organic ad was the original organic ad, with simple white text on a dark-colored navy background. Our treatment for this experiment was the same simple white text but placed on a brighter color teal background. We predict that the brighter color background can create a sense of urgency and stand out to the Facebook user, leading to an increase in click-through rate. The copy on both ads is the same.
This was an A/B test with 50/50 split in traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using a brighter color background for the Facebook ad did not perform better in terms of Click-Through Rate (CTR) and Cost Per Click (CPC). The treatment group, which used the brighter color background, actually saw a decrease in clicks compared to the control group.

It is important to note that the statistical level of confidence for this decrease in clicks was only 10.1%, which did not validate since it was not above a 95% level of confidence.

In future experimentation, it may be beneficial to test other variables or factors that could impact CTR and CPC, such as ad copy - this can include ad copy length, font, size, and even emojis/symbols.

experiments.title: How concise copy and additional clarity impacted donor conversions for a content offer
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that simplifying the language, reordering information for a more logical flow, and emphasizing key points for visitors will achieve an increase in clarity and comprehension.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/05/2024 - 01/16/2024
In an effort to increase the donor conversion rate on IMB’s Prayer Calendar campaign, we decided to run a copy test to condense some of the copy. Our hypothesis was that the more concise the copy, the higher level of comprehension for the potential donor therefore, leading them to the donation form quicker.
We believe that simplifying the language, reordering information for a more logical flow, and emphasizing key points will lead to increased clarity and comprehension. As a result, we believe the treatment version will outperform the control version in terms of donor understanding and response, leading to a higher conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that simplifying the language, reordering information for a more logical flow, and emphasizing key points in the copy had a very slight lift in the number of transactions. While the treatment version of the campaign showed a 1.1% increase in transactions, the statistical level of confidence was only 3.1%, falling below the recommended threshold of 95% for validity.

Furthermore, we saw a a drop of 35.3% in revenue between the control and treatment at a 12.1% level of confidence. While this did not validate, it is important to consider in future decision making that IMB's donors give less when they are given less value proposition, and more concise copy may lead to a lower average gift.

In future experimentation, it is important to carry out the A/B test for a longer duration to gather a larger sample size and increase the statistical power. This will help establish a more reliable and accurate conclusion about the effectiveness of the treatment version. Additionally, it may be beneficial to further refine and optimize the treatment version based on the insights gained from this experiment. Overall, the learnings from this experiment should be used as a starting point for further iterations and testing to drive improved results.

experiments.title: How loss aversion language on a donation page impacted donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that leveraging the psychological concept of loss aversion for potential donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because the emphasis on their contribution will create a stronger sense of urgency and connection.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/16/2024 - 02/02/2024
In an effort to increase our conversion rate on IMB’s Prayer Calendar donation page, we wanted to test the inclusion of loss aversion language to see how their audience would respond.
Our hypothesis was that by leveraging the psychological concept of loss aversion, we believe that potential donors will be more motivated to take action. Specifically, we hypothesize that framing the donation as an opportunity to prevent missed chances and emphasizing the tangible impact of their contribution will create a stronger sense of urgency and connection. This heightened emotional response is expected to increase donor conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that leveraging the psychological concept of loss aversion did not lead to the desired increase in donor conversion rate. The treatment group, which included loss aversion language, actually saw a decrease in transactions compared to the control group as well as an even larger decrease in revenue and average gift.

While this experiment did not validate, it was trending enough in a negative direction that we decided to turn it off. It can be concluded that IMB's audience did not respond well to the loss aversion language.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to explore other strategies or messaging approaches to increase donor conversion rates. Additionally, it may be helpful to conduct further research and analysis to understand why the loss aversion language did not have the expected effect. This will allow for more informed decision making and potential adjustments to the messaging strategy in future experiments.

experiments.title: How more relevant copy on a content offer landing page impacts email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using relevant and specific copy for Month specific Prayer Calendar landing page visiotors will achieve our email acqusition rate because there would be more relevancy and an inside look at what is inside the calendar for that month.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/08/2024 - 08/05/2024
IMB has a Prayer Calendar offer that offers monthly prayer prompts. While we have used the entire calendar to market the product, we decided to focus on month-to-month as well to see if the relevancy would increase our email acquisition rate.
On the month-to-month landing page, we tested using a copy change that included the contents of the July prayer prompt versus the general copy used on the main funnel.
We ran this A/B test for about a month with the traffic split evenly between the two versions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that while using relevant and specific copy for the Month-specific Prayer Calendar landing page seemed like a promising idea, the results did not support the hypothesis. The treatment, which included the contents of the July prayer prompt, actually saw a decrease in Email Acquisition for All Traffic compared to the control group.

It is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was only 17.2%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it would be advisable to conduct further tests with a larger sample size and potentially refine the specific copy used for the Month-specific Prayer Calendar landing page. It may be worth exploring different approaches to see if there are ways to leverage the content of the calendar for increased email acquisition without negatively impacting the results. Additionally, considering other factors such as the design of the landing page, call-to-action placement, and overall user experience could also help in optimizing the effectiveness of the campaign.

experiments.title: How an embed form impacts donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using an embedded donation form for the Bible Direct Ask Donation Page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because there would be less visual friction within the donation form. because there would be less visual friction within the donation form.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/09/2024
IMB uses a custom frame donation form for all donation pages. In an effort to optimize their donation process, we decided to test using an embedded form that did include the styling of their custom frame. Our goal was that by removing the styling, we would see an increase in donor conversion metrics due to the more natural-looking donation form process. We tested this split URL test on their Bible Direct Ask offer called “God’s Word for the Nations” with equal traffic going to both the custom frame and the embedded form.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using an embedded donation form on the Bible Direct Ask Funnel resulted in only a 5.7% lift in transactions at a level of confidence of 15.3%, indicating these results are invalid.

In terms of revenue, the control produced $3,555 in revenue and the treatment produced $1930. Therefore the control had an average gift of $148 and the treatment produced an average gift of $77. This produced a 44.8% drop in revenue at a 65.3% level of confidence, also considered invalid.

Due to the lack of validity in results, these results indicate that the embedded made little to no difference in the donor conversion metrics for IMB's audience.

In future experimentation, we plan to continue testing ways to optimize IMB's donation process and increase our donor conversion metrics for the organization.

experiments.title: How a higher contrast donation button impacts clicks and overall giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a higher contrast button on the donate button for homepage visitors will achieve an increase in clicks and overall giving because the higher contrast button would attract the visitors attention quicker therefore reducing friction on the page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/03/2024 - 05/29/2024
In an effort to push more traffic to IMB’s Home Donation Page, we tested using a higher contrast button to attract visitor’s attention.
This type of test has resulted in a lift in donor conversion metrics for other clients, so we were excited to see how it performed.
Our goal was that by using a higher contrast button color, more visitors would click the button to be directed to the donation page, therefore, increasing giving.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using a higher contrast button on the donate button for homepage visitors resulted in a 4.3% increase in transactions for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 27.3%, which means that these results are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence. Additionally, it may be beneficial to run the experiment for a longer period of time to gather more data and potentially increase the validity of the results.

Overall, while the initial results show promise, it is essential to rely on statistically significant data to make informed decisions about implementing changes to the website.

experiments.title: How a headline with more clarity and impact bullet points affected donation page performance
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the addition of value proposition and impact for visitors will achieve an increase in performance on the donation page because visitors would have a more tangible understanding of the impact of their donation.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/21/2023 - 12/06/2023
In an effort to increase the donor conversion rate on our Holy Lands funnel for IMB, we decided to run a 3-way split test. We ran the control page vs. a new headline, vs the new headline + impact bullet points.
Variation 1:
New headline: Reach hurting people in the Holy Land with the transformative power of the gospel
Variation 2:
New headline: Reach hurting people in the Holy Land with the transformative power of the gospel
Impact bullet points:
$10, you’ll purchase a Bible for one person in their local language.
$30, you’ll pay for one person to receive one hour of trauma counseling.
$100, you’ll pay for a hosted training event in trauma healing leadership.
$200, you’ll pay for one month of media outreach.
We thought that the headline would offer additional clarity and value proposition as well as the impact bullet points which detailed. out exactly what each gift amount would result in.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning of this experiment is that the new headline had a negative impact of 47.2% on the number of transactions while the new headline and impact bullets had a very minimal increase on the number of transactions.
While these results are not what we hypothesized, neither of the variations validated, so we can not rule out the effectiveness of the headline change or addition of the impact bullets.
We did notice that the impact bullets were based on these amounts $10, $30, $100, $200, but the gift array on this donation page had these amounts pre-selected: $100,$250,$500,$1,000. It would be interesting to run this test again focusing only on the impact bullets, but match up the impact bullet amounts with the gift array. We hypothesize that the stark difference between the options given and the impact bullets could have caused additional friction.
An important note: revenue is not pulling into Winston
However, VWO is reporting that Variation 1 raised $5,125 in revenue and Variation 2 raised $1,055. Based on these results, we did make the decision to roll out the new headline to all traffic.
It is crucial that we retest the use of a clear but impact based headline as well as impact bullets.

experiments.title: How a large image on a landing page affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing the hero image for Holy Lands donation page visitors will achieve an increase in conversion rate because it will reduce visual friction on the page.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/31/2023
The IMB was running a campaign to raise funds for mission work in the Holy Lands during the Israel-Hamas conflict in 2023. Motivation to give was high for the campaign, reflected in high conversion rates even from direct acquisition.
When looking for opportunities to optimize conversion, the team wondered if the image on the landing page — although striking — might actually be pushing the rest of the content too far down on the page. It was large on the desktop version, and pushed all the copy, value proposition, and donation form below the initial “fold”.
They quickly mocked up an experiment that removed the image and launched an A/B test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that removing the hero image from the Holy Lands donation page resulted in a 9.5% increase in donations. However, the statistical level of confidence is below the recommended threshold of 85%, so these results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.
Based on this, it is important to recognize that the experiment did show a potential impact on conversions by removing the hero image. This suggests that reducing visual friction on the page could be beneficial in improving conversion rates.
To further validate these findings and make informed decisions, future experimentation should be conducted with a larger sample size and higher statistical level of confidence. This will help ensure the accuracy and reliability of the results.
Additionally, it is recommended to explore other potential optimizations on the landing page to further improve conversion rates. This could involve testing different layout designs, call-to-action placements, or content positioning to find the most effective approach for encouraging donations.
Overall, the learnings from this experiment highlight the importance of conducting rigorous and statistically valid experiments to make informed decisions in future optimization efforts.

experiments.title: How lowering a gift array on an instant donation page for an acquisition offer affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that lowering the gift array for note of encouragement signers will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the lower gift array aligns better with the perceived value of the offer.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/21/2023
IMB is running an acquisition offer through paid Facebook ads to sign a note of encouragement for missionaries who are traveling and out in the field. They wanted to increase the donor conversion rate on the instant donation page of the acquisition offer so we decided to lower the gift array because this is a low value offer and they aren’t receiving in return like an ebook funnel.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that lowering the gift array for note of encouragement signers did not achieve a higher donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment version with the lower gift array saw a significant decrease in donations of 49.3% compared to the control version.
Based on these results, it is evident that the perceived value of the offer did not align with the lower gift array. This suggests that donors may be more willing to donate higher amounts when presented with a wider range of options. Or it suggests that users see this content offer as high value and makes them want to give more to support more missionaries.
In future experimentation, it is important to consider the perceived value of the offer and ensure that the gift array aligns with this value. It may be beneficial to test different gift array options to find the optimal balance between a wide range of options and the perceived value.

experiments.title: How a Security Callout on a Landing Page Impacts Sign-Up Rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a security callout at the bottom of the form for users on the Course on Prayer landing page will achieve an increase in email acquisitions because it removes anxiety around giving up an email. because  it removes anxiety around giving up an email.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/25/2023 - 06/25/2023
The IMB’s Course on Prayer offer was experiencing a low sign-up rate on the landing page. We hypothesized that eliminating anxiety with a simple security callout at the bottom of the form would reassure users by clarifying what their information will be used for. We believe a reminder to the user of why their email is being requested (to receive their course lessons) will increase the number of emails acquired.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results of this experiment should not be considered valid due to the statistical level of confidence being below 85%. Ultimately, a very small lift with a low level of confidence shows that this treatment did not cause much of a difference for users arriving at this landing page. More testing is required, but this also prompts us to explore what other factors could be causing anxiety/friction to email acquisition.
In future experimentation, it is crucial to have a larger sample size and a higher level of confidence before making any decisions based on the results. It may also be beneficial to test alternative forms of security callouts or messaging to determine which resonates best with visitors.

experiments.title: How adding a recurring giving pop up during the giving process impacts monthly gifts
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a recurring giving interruptor  for those making a one time gift on the main donate page will achieve an increase in monthly giving because we are adding clarity as to why someone should make a recurring gift instead of a one-time gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/02/2023 - 05/18/2023
In an effort to increase the number of monthly donors, we wanted to prompt more people to give a monthly gift on the IMB’s main donation page. After the first step of the giving process (selecting one time + gift amount + selecting “Give Now”), they would receive a pop up that asked people to change their gift frequency to monthly. They would receive a custom ask amount based off of their original one-time gift amount. If their gift was over $200, they did not receive this prompt.
Our hypothesis is that this prompt would draw more attention to the monthly option and provide additional value proposition language as to why someone should make a monthly gift as opposed to a one-time gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment aimed to increase the number of monthly donors on IMB's main donation page by adding a pop-up interruptor that prompted people to change their gift frequency to monthly after the first step of the giving process. The hypothesis was that this would draw more attention to the monthly option and provide additional value proposition language as to why someone should make a monthly gift as opposed to a one-time gift.
The experiment ran for 15 days and showed that the treatment group saw a 27.6% increase in Monthly Donations with a 39.6% statistical level of confidence. This means that this resulted in virtually no difference in monthly gifts.
But, we did see a valid decrease in overall gifts. A 35% decrease with a level of confidence of 99%. This data point tells us that the pop up was not motivating enough  for people to make a monthly gift, but disruptive enough for people to potentially abandon the giving process.
This experiment will be retested in a different channel - an instant donation page in an offer funnel. It could be that motivations are different enough for prospective donors that we would see different results.

experiments.title: How adding clarity and exclusivity to a newsletter signup footer impacts email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including value prop about why to sign up for communications from IMB for for users on the homepage will achieve an increased email conversion rate because people need to see value in signing up if they are going to provide their personal email because  people need to see value in signing up if they are going to provide their personal email..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/24/2023 - 05/06/2023
At the bottom of IMB’s homepage is a call to sign up for communications and a form for users to submit their email.  We hypothesized that adding more value prop copy to the Sign-Up—verbiage about why should someone sign up, what will you receive, etc—would increase email acquisition. The control copy was shorter, but more general, leveraging with a grab about a newsletter with interesting stories. Alternatively, the treatment tested more copy, but conveyed more details about what you’d receive and why to sign up.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The statistical level of confidence is not high enough to consider these results valid. Future experimentation should be conducted with a larger sample size given that the differences observed were under 1%. The experiment resulted in a 6% decrease in email conversion rate with a level of confidence of 33%.
One idea to execute a test of this hypothesis with a validating result is a more prominent treatment. This Sign Up form is located at the very bottom of IMB's homepage which limits the pool size only to those who scroll to the bottom. This same copy could be tested with a slideout or popup that appears on the first portion of the page.

experiments.title: How addressing a felt need in landing page copy impacts email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that addressing the felt need for prospective ebook downloaders will achieve a higher email acquisition rate  because relevancy to the individual’s needs strengthens the value proposition for the offer.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/28/2023 - 04/13/2023
IMB was experiencing a relatively low sign up rate on an offer landing page. To address this, we developed a treatment for this landing page that would better address a person’s felt need. The control for the experiment starts the page with a declarative statement to take action. The treatment invites the person to think about why they would want to sign up for this online course and provides ways that this offer will fulfill those needs. Our hypothesis is that the language that unpacks the “why” would result in more emails acquired.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that addressing the felt need of prospective customers can lead to a higher email acquisition rate. The treatment that focused on the "why" behind signing up for the online course instead of just encouraging action saw a 4.4% increase in email acquisitions compared to the control group.
However, the statistical level of confidence was only 61.3%, which means that these results cannot be considered valid for decision making.
Additionally, downstream we saw that donor conversion rate was negatively affected by the copy (though also not a valid result).
It's likely that this low impact is because the felt need is already addressed at the ad level (where most traffic to this page is coming from). Therefore, people weren't more motivated by this language on this landing page.

experiments.title: How adding a campaign name in an email appeal affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a familiar campaign name for email recipients will achieve a higher donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/30/2022
The IMB historically runs a Lottie Moon Christmas Offering campaign and they communicate this fund by name in all their communications. When developing emails for the year end campaign there was initially no mention of the Lottie Moon Christmas Offering. We wanted to see if adding the name of this campaign would impact giving for IMB’s housefile list (who have historically known about this campaign). We tested this verbiage in one email appeal in their campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment resulted in a 15% increase in donor conversion rate with only a level of confidence of 35%. However, when looking at the impact on revenue, we saw a 360% directional lift with a level of confidence of 93%. However, there were several larger $1k+ gifts in the treatment. Nonetheless, this could be an indication that those who are familiar with the Lottie Moon offering may give larger gifts because of the familiarity of this campaign. It should also be noted that mid-level and major donors were suppressed from this campaign so it is likely that some donors upgraded their giving in this appeal and gave larger gifts than they typically would. It is recommended that this is tested across multiple emails in the future in order to confirm that while it may not affect donor conversion rate, it may impact the average gift in an appeal.

experiments.title: How adding an offer to subscription form impacts email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that offering a free eBook for signups on the homepage will achieve an increase in email acquisition..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/30/2022 - 10/13/2022
IMB’s homepage has an email signup form at the bottom of the page. It encourages users to Sign Up Today to receive emails with transformative stories and highlights of the work the organization is doing. We hypothesized that offering a free eBook in place of this would lead to an increase in email acquisition. This was for two reasons. First, the original signup copy can at first seem a little organization-focused. Is the user just signing up for a standard weekly update? Second, we believe the free offer communicates greater value immediately by providing them with a free ebook; it also makes the implication that signing up would ensure you receive future offers like this. Value is provided immediately, but there is an emphasis on the value of becoming a subscriber.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment ran 44 days, but despite a strong number of conversions for the data, ultimately the results did not validate with confidence — at only 38.7%
Regardless, the increase in conversion rate was very slight at 1.4% for all traffic. Notably, mobile visitors did see a valid 45.5% increase at a 97% level of confidence. Ultimately, more testing is required in order to determine results.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Mobile visitors had a 45.5% increase in email acquisitions with a 97% level of confidence.Organic Search visitors had a 65.2% increase in email acquisitions with an 89% level of confidence.Referral visitors had a 103.1% increase in email acquisitions with an 86% level of confidence.(Other) visitors had a 75.6% decrease in email acquisitions with a 99% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How adding a humanized sticky bar to a main donation page impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a personalized sticky bar for main donation page visitors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/08/2022 - 07/31/2022
In efforts to increase the donor conversion rate on the main donation page, IMB added a humanized sticky bar at the top of their page. This sticky bar would not have a call to action and would just be a preemptive “thank you” from Paul Chitwood (IMB’s President). Our hypothesis was that adding a message from Paul Chitwood would remind the prospective donor that they’re hearing from a person, not just an organization. This would in turn, increase donor conversion rate on this page.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for 20 days, the treatment made no impact on conversion rate. This experiment resulted in a 6% decrease in conversion rate but with only a level of confidence of 57%. Meaning, half the the time it will result in a 6% decrease, and half the time it will not. Our conclusion is that those who are coming to the main donation page already have a high level of motivation. They're deliberately coming to this page to make a donation and therefore may not be impacted by a thank you message from Paul Chitwood. As a follow up test, we'd like to add a message from Paul Chitwood with value proposition-based language on the actual donation page.
